notes: Round 9 human-idea audit after the round-8 B-feed experiment: Tiling stays accepted because the 64x384 hot band is still the measured macro-shape sweet spot; Coalescing Access, Data Reuse, Async Copy, and Pg2s remain baseline infrastructure; L2 Cache stays rejected because the grouped CTA remap already regressed runtime and hot-band time; Stage stays rejected because the fixed-shape control refactor also regressed; the round-8 warp-local B consumer transform did not beat the accepted base, but it did produce the first meaningful same-family positive signal after those failures: runtime improved by about 0.65 ms over the negative L2 run, hot-band time dropped to about 41.17 us, tensor active rose to about 38.47, short scoreboard improved, `mio_throttle` collapsed to about 0.33, registers stayed at 167, and shared footprint did not grow. That is enough evidence to keep the last two rounds on the B-feed family rather than switching again. Recommended direction dir_01 therefore advances from consumer issue order alone to the user's deeper producer/consumer-layout split inside the same shared-memory budget: keep the new single-fragment streaming consumer path, but physically permute the B shared layout inside each warp group so producer layout and consumer layout are no longer identical.

directions.name: Human idea two-level B staging follow-through: footprint-neutral B shared permutation on top of the current streaming consumer path
directions.hypothesis: Round 8 suggests the B-feed family is finally moving the right metrics without raising registers or shared memory, but issue-order alone is not enough. The next step is the user's earlier producer-layout / consumer-layout split, done inside the existing footprint: keep the current single-fragment mirrored consumer path, but change the physical placement of B chunks in shared memory so `stage_b_shared_tile_async` writes a bank-friendlier order and `b_shared_col_from_logical` maps logical consumer columns back onto that permuted layout. This keeps macro tiling, stage depth, and cp.async volume fixed while making the producer and consumer views of B deliberately different.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local shared/L1 operand delivery and bank behavior on the hot-band B fragment path rather than CTA-level control flow.
directions.risk: Moderate. This is still correctness-sensitive, and the shared permutation has to preserve 16-byte async-copy alignment plus the current footprint. The guardrail is strict: no second B shared tile, no CTA repack, no extra barrier, no stage-depth loss.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if the shared permutation increases footprint, complicates into CTA repack, or leaves hot-band time flat. The family only stays alive while it is shared-neutral and warp-local.

directions.name: Warp-specialized producer/consumer on top of the current B-feed branch
directions.hypothesis: If the final rounds need a materially different branch while staying close to the current positive signal, the next orchestration family is still producer/consumer warp specialization. Apply it on top of the current streaming B-consumer branch rather than the old baseline: let one small producer subset handle staging while the remaining warps stay primarily on MMA. This tests whether the remaining gap is now orchestration rather than raw operand order.
directions.expected_bottleneck: All-warps staging and handoff overhead after the most obvious B-fragment issue pressure has already been reduced.
directions.risk: High. The kernel is still occupancy-limited by registers, so giving up compute warps can easily erase any overlap gain. This is better treated as the backup branch if dir_01 stalls immediately.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if barrier stall drops but runtime and hot-band time do not, or if tensor active falls because too many warps stopped doing MMA.

directions.name: Human idea Ps2r on the current streaming branch: one-fragment shared-to-register lookahead
directions.hypothesis: The current branch already streams one B fragment at a time in mirrored order. The next direct feed-latency bet is to overlap the next B fragment load from shared to registers with the current MMA issue chain, effectively turning the new single-fragment path into a one-step Ps2r pipeline. This is the user's Ps2r idea applied after the round-8 consumer refactor rather than on the older pair-load path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual shared-to-register feed latency inside the hot-band 64x64 micro-tile after the first consumer-order cleanup.
directions.risk: High register risk. The branch is still sitting on the register wall, so a Ps2r lookahead can quickly wipe out the small signal round 8 recovered.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if registers climb or occupancy worsens without a clear hot-band runtime win.
